statement: EU/EEA nationals working in Iceland are concentrated in specific sectors. As of 2025, tourism and hospitality employed approximately 25–30% of EEA foreign workers, construction 20–25%, fish processing 10–15%, and healthcare 5–8%. Polish nationals are the largest single group, comprising roughly 35% of all EEA workers. Approximately 42,000 EEA/EU nationals held valid residence registrations in Iceland in 2025. The sectoral concentration has shifted over time: before 2015, fish processing and construction dominated, while the tourism boom (2015–2019) drew large numbers into accommodation, food services, and transport.
source_name: Hagstofa Íslands — Foreign citizens by industry and citizenship; Vinnumálastofnun
caveats: Sectoral breakdowns rely on ÍSAT industry classifications. Short-term and seasonal workers may be under-counted in registry data. Self-employed EEA nationals are harder to track by sector.

related_entries.statement: Iceland already participates in the free movement of workers through the EEA Agreement, which has been in force since 1994. EEA nationals have the right to live and work in Iceland without work permits, and Icelanders have reciprocal rights across the EU/EEA. EU membership would not create new free movement rights beyond what already exists under the EEA framework. However, EU membership would give Iceland voting rights in shaping the rules governing free movement, whereas under the EEA Iceland implements EU single market legislation without a formal vote in the Council or European Parliament.

Since EEA membership took effect in 1994, Iceland's foreign-born population has grown substantially, from approximately 2% of the total population to roughly 18% by 2025 (~70,000 people out of ~390,000). The largest immigrant groups are from Poland (~20,000), Lithuania (~5,000), and the Philippines (~4,000). EEA/EU nationals account for approximately 60% of all foreign residents. Immigration accelerated sharply after 2004 EU enlargement opened labour markets to new member states, and again during Iceland's tourism boom (2015–2019).
